title: Sentiment Analysis
subtitle: Teaching Machines to Feel the Vibe!
overview: This session introduces Natural Language Processing (NLP) through one of its most popular applications — Sentiment Analysis. Students discover how machines read and understand human language, classify emotions in text, and power real-world tools like product review analysers, social media monitors, and customer feedback systems.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — NLP pipeline diagram, tokenisation example, word cloud, positive/negative review examples, VADER score output screenshot.

keyTopics: What is NLP? — Natural Language Processing: teaching machines to read, understand, and generate human language
Sentiment Analysis — Classifying text as Positive, Negative, or Neutral based on emotional tone
NLP Pipeline — Raw Text → Tokenise → Clean → Remove Stopwords → Vectorise → Model → Predict
Tokenisation — Splitting text into individual words or subword units for processing
Stopwords — Common words (the, is, and) that carry no sentiment and are removed before analysis
Bag of Words & TF-IDF — Converting text into numerical vectors that machine learning models can process
VADER Sentiment Analyser — Rule-based Python tool that scores text sentiment without training data
Real-World Applications — Product reviews, social media monitoring, customer support, stock market prediction

activity.title: Movie Review Sentiment Analyser — VADER + Python + Word Cloud
parts: Google Colab (colab.research.google.com) — free, no install needed
NLTK and VADER (install in Colab with pip)
Sample movie review dataset (provided by teacher or from NLTK built-in corpus)
steps: Open Google Colab and install libraries: !pip install nltk vaderSentiment wordcloud
Import: from vaderSentiment.vaderSentiment import SentimentIntensityAnalyzer, import pandas as pd, from wordcloud import WordCloud, import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
Test on sample sentences: analyzer.polarity_scores('This movie was absolutely amazing!') — read the neg, neu, pos, compound scores
Load the movie review dataset: df = pd.read_csv('movie_reviews.csv') and display df.head()
Apply VADER to every review: df['compound'] = df['review'].apply(lambda x: analyzer.polarity_scores(x)['compound'])
Classify: df['sentiment'] = df['compound'].apply(lambda x: 'Positive' if x >= 0.05 else ('Negative' if x <= -0.05 else 'Neutral'))
Plot a bar chart showing count of Positive, Negative, and Neutral reviews
Generate a Word Cloud from all positive reviews — visualise the most common positive words
Challenge: Compare VADER results against the actual labels in the dataset — calculate accuracy and discuss where VADER gets it wrong!

options: A technique for processing images using neural networks
A field of AI that enables computers to read, understand, analyse, and generate human language
A method for speeding up Python code execution
A type of database used to store text documents
quiz.explanation: NLP = computers understanding human language! It powers autocomplete, translation, chatbots, spam filters, voice assistants, and sentiment analysis. Language is complex — sarcasm, slang, context — NLP teaches machines to handle all of it!

options: Analysing the grammar and spelling of a text document
Automatically identifying and classifying the emotional tone of text as Positive, Negative, or Neutral
Translating text from one language to another
Summarising long documents into short bullet points
quiz.explanation: Sentiment Analysis = reading the vibe! Given a product review, tweet, or comment, the model decides: is this person happy (Positive), unhappy (Negative), or indifferent (Neutral)? Used by every major company to monitor customer opinions at scale!

options: Converting text into a secret encrypted code
Removing all punctuation from a sentence
Splitting raw text into individual units — usually words or subwords — for processing
Counting the total number of characters in a document
quiz.explanation: Tokenisation = chopping text into pieces! 'I love Python' → ['I', 'love', 'Python']. Machines cannot process a whole sentence at once — they need individual tokens. Every NLP pipeline starts with tokenisation!

options: Words that cause the NLP model to stop processing
The most important emotional words in a sentence
Common, frequently occurring words like 'the', 'is', 'and' that carry no meaningful sentiment and are removed before analysis
Words that appear only once in the entire dataset
quiz.explanation: Stopwords = noise words! 'The movie is good' — 'The' and 'is' add no sentiment. Removing them leaves 'movie good' — cleaner signal for the model. NLTK has a built-in stopwords list for 20+ languages!

quiz.q: What is the BAG OF WORDS model?
options: A physical container used to store training data
A text representation that counts how many times each word appears in a document — ignoring grammar and word order
A neural network layer that processes words one at a time
A technique for generating new sentences from training data
quiz.explanation: Bag of Words = word frequency counter! Each document becomes a vector of word counts. 'I love this movie I love it' → {I:2, love:2, this:1, movie:1, it:1}. Simple but powerful — loses word order but captures vocabulary presence for classification!

options: A deep learning technique for image recognition
Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency — a scoring method that gives higher weight to words that are frequent in a document but rare across all documents
A method for translating text between languages
A type of neural network used for text generation
quiz.explanation: TF-IDF = smart word scoring! TF = how often a word appears in THIS document. IDF = how rare it is across ALL documents. 'Brilliant' appearing in one review scores high. 'The' appears everywhere — low score. Identifies truly meaningful words!

quiz.q: What is VADER in sentiment analysis?
options: A deep learning model trained on millions of movie reviews
A rule-based sentiment analysis tool specifically designed for social media text — it scores text without needing any training
A Python library for visualising text data
A tokeniser that splits text into subword units
quiz.explanation: VADER = Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner! It uses a hand-crafted dictionary of words with sentiment scores. No training needed — just install and score. Great for social media, emojis, slang, and ALL CAPS emphasis!

quiz.q: What does the COMPOUND score in VADER represent?
options: The total number of words in the analysed text
A normalised overall sentiment score ranging from -1.0 (most negative) to +1.0 (most positive)
The percentage of neutral words in the text
The confidence level of the sentiment prediction
quiz.explanation: Compound = overall verdict! VADER gives neg, neu, pos scores for proportions, then a compound score: +1.0 = maximally positive, -1.0 = maximally negative, 0 = neutral. Rule of thumb: compound ≥ 0.05 = Positive, ≤ -0.05 = Negative!

quiz.q: What is a WORD CLOUD used for in NLP analysis?
options: Storing words in a compressed cloud database
Training a sentiment model faster using GPU cloud computing
Visualising word frequency — the larger the word appears, the more often it occurs in the dataset
Removing duplicate words from a text corpus
quiz.explanation: Word Cloud = frequency visualisation! Words that appear most often are displayed largest. Generate one from positive reviews and you instantly see dominant words like 'amazing', 'love', 'perfect'. Great for quick exploratory analysis!

quiz.q: Why might a rule-based tool like VADER struggle with SARCASM?
options: VADER cannot process sentences longer than 10 words
VADER scores each word individually based on its dictionary value — sarcasm like 'Oh great, another delay!' uses positive words but means the opposite
VADER only works on positive text
VADER requires internet access to detect sarcasm
quiz.explanation: Sarcasm = NLP's nightmare! 'Oh brilliant, my flight is delayed again' uses the positive word 'brilliant' — VADER scores it positive. But the meaning is negative! Context and tone are lost in word-by-word scoring. Deep learning models handle sarcasm better.

quiz.q: What does lambda x: analyzer.polarity_scores(x)['compound'] do in Python?
options: Trains a new sentiment model on the dataset
Applies the VADER analyser to each row in a DataFrame column and extracts the compound score
Removes all neutral reviews from the dataset
Converts text reviews into numerical token IDs
quiz.explanation: Lambda + apply = row-by-row transformation! For every review text x in the column, it runs VADER and extracts the compound score. Result: a new column of sentiment scores for the entire dataset — processed in one clean line!

quiz.q: What are the THREE main sentiment categories in sentiment analysis?
options: Happy, Sad, Angry
Positive, Negative, Neutral
High, Medium, Low
True, False, Unknown
quiz.explanation: Positive (happy, praising, approving), Negative (unhappy, criticising, complaining), Neutral (factual, indifferent, no strong emotion). Some advanced systems go deeper — Very Positive, Positive, Neutral, Negative, Very Negative — or detect specific emotions like joy, anger, fear!

quiz.q: Which company use case is a perfect real-world application of sentiment analysis?
options: Google Maps calculating the fastest driving route
Netflix encoding video streams for faster playback
Amazon automatically scanning millions of product reviews to identify negative trends and flag products for quality review
Spotify compressing audio files to save storage space
quiz.explanation: Amazon + Sentiment Analysis = millions of reviews processed automatically! Instead of humans reading every review, NLP scans all of them instantly — flagging products with rising negative sentiment, spotting fake reviews, and surfacing top complaints. Scale impossible for humans!

quiz.q: What is the difference between LEXICON-BASED and MACHINE LEARNING-BASED sentiment analysis?
options: Lexicon-based is faster but less accurate; ML-based is always slower and less accurate
Lexicon-based uses a pre-built dictionary of sentiment words and rules; ML-based learns sentiment patterns from labelled training data
Lexicon-based requires a GPU; ML-based runs on any computer
They produce identical results using different programming languages
quiz.explanation: Lexicon-based (like VADER) = dictionary of pre-scored words + rules. Fast, no training needed, works out of the box. ML-based (like Naive Bayes, LSTM) = learns from thousands of labelled examples. More accurate on domain-specific text but needs labelled data to train!

quiz.q: What is a CORPUS in NLP?
options: A single sentence used to test a sentiment model
A large structured collection of text data used for training or evaluating NLP models
The output label predicted by a sentiment classifier
A Python function that tokenises text
quiz.explanation: Corpus = text dataset! The NLTK movie reviews corpus has 2000 labelled reviews (1000 positive, 1000 negative). IMDB corpus has 50,000. Training NLP models requires large, diverse corpora — the bigger and more varied, the better the model learns language!
